prompt: Be honest with your maker. The last time you had a fitness-adjacent goal, what happened?

options.label: I went 4 times a week, tracking every macro like a scientist.
I bought a yoga mat, used it once as a stretching pad, and it is now gathering dust under my bed.
I paid for a membership for 8 months without going because I was too polite to cancel.
I run outside until my knees click, then stop for three months.

prompt: It is Tuesday at 6:45 PM. It is dark, slightly drizzling, and you just finished a brutal work meeting. The gym is a 12-minute drive away. What are you doing?

options.label: Driving there. The iron calls to me.
Sitting in my car in the driveway for 25 minutes scrolling TikTok, eventually eating cereal.
I would go, but only if I have a pre-workout drink that makes my face itch so badly I have to lift things.
Opening a bottle of wine and doing three aggressive air squats to alleviate the guilt.

prompt: How do you feel about making accidental eye contact with a stranger who is grunting loudly while doing biceps curls directly in front of the mirror?

options.label: Standard Tuesday behavior. I will nod respectfully.
I would rather swallow a live wasp than navigate the social anxiety of a crowded free-weight section.
I only care if the lighting is optimal for my post-workout pump selfie.
I prefer to sweat in the dark, isolated privacy of my own existential dread.

prompt: What does your ideal workout environment smell like?

options.label: Chalk, stale sweat, and iron.
Eucalyptus, expensive candles, and a $16 cold-pressed juice.
My own living room, specifically because nobody else is there.
I don't know, what does a Netflix binge smell like?

prompt: If you don't show up for a workout, what happens?

options.label: I feel a deep, internal shame that ruins my day.
I am financially penalized $35 by my boutique studio app.
Absolutely nothing. The corporate gym chain quietly thanks me for my donation.
I just re-schedule it to 'tomorrow' for the fourth time this week.

prompt: If you were forced to buy one piece of fitness equipment today, what are you getting?

options.label: A sleek, pastel-colored kettlebell that matches my aesthetic.
A power rack. I'm building a sanctuary in my garage.
Liquid chalk and some lifting straps.
Does a massage gun count?

prompt: What is your typical workout attire?

options.label: Whatever is clean. Usually an old t-shirt from a 2014 fun run.
A matching $120 set that looks great under neon studio lighting.
Stringer tank top or oversized pump cover.
The same sweatpants I've been wearing since yesterday morning.

prompt: What are you listening to while 'working out'?

options.label: Hardstyle, metal, or a podcast about optimizing my sleep protocol.
A perfectly curated deep-house playlist provided by the instructor.
True crime podcasts, but I'm actually just doing chores around the house.
Whatever top 40 pop is blasting from the gym's overhead speakers.

prompt: You just finished a session. What's next?

options.label: Chugging a chalky protein shake in my car.
Paying $16 for a smoothie containing ingredients I can't pronounce.
Lying on my living room floor contemplating my life choices.
Taking a long shower and convincing myself I deserve pizza.

prompt: It's raining. Your planned workout is ruined. Why?

options.label: It's not. I'm already at the gym.
I can't be bothered to drive. I'll just do pushups in my bedroom.
My spin class got cancelled. Guess I'll get brunch instead.
Raining? That's prime blanket weather. Try again in spring.

prompt: An elderly stranger is completely naked, drying their toes with a hair dryer directly in your line of sight. Your immediate reaction is to:

options.label: Ignore it. I came here to bleed under a barbell, not judge a senior citizen's post-shower routine.
Feel completely vindicated in my decision to exercise 4 feet from my own bed.
Silently vow to report this to management while adjusting my silk head wrap.
Use this mental trauma as a valid excuse to skip working out for the next three business weeks.

prompt: You notice a $45 monthly charge for a fitness app you haven't opened since the Obama administration. What is your next move?

options.label: Keep paying it because 'next month is when I turn my life around' (spoiler: you won't).
Cancel it instantly and buy a second-hand kettlebell off Facebook Marketplace.
Upgrade to the premium tier because it comes with an aesthetic meal plan PDF you'll never look at.
Transfer that capital directly into your commercial gym membership where you actually lift heavy things.

prompt: How do you feel about being completely drenched in sweat to the point where your clothes stick to your body?

options.label: It's the ultimate proof of dominance over my physical limitations. Wipe it on the shirt and keep going.
It is an unholy biohazard. If I cannot immediately step into a tiled shower with luxury botanical soaps, I will perish.
Completely fine, as long as no other human being is within a 5-mile radius to witness my damp misery.
Absolutely disgusting. Sweat is a design flaw. I actively avoid any movement that raises my core temperature.

prompt: An influencer is filming a TikTok dance right in front of the weight rack you need. What do you do?

options.label: Walk directly through their frame to grab the 45-pound plates. My gains do not yield to social media algorithms.
Leave the room entirely and retreat to my basement bunker where the internet cannot hurt me.
Slightly adjust my high-waisted leggings and try to naturally blend into the background of their video.
Watch them from the comfort of a machine I’m sitting on without actually exercising, using it as a distraction.

prompt: It's drizzling outside. Not a monsoon, just a light, depressing gray mist. How does this affect your workout plan?

options.label: The perfect atmospheric setting to put on a dark hoodie, blast heavy metal, and lift heavy metal.
I'm already in my living room, so the sky's emotional problems are completely irrelevant to me.
Cancel my $35 spin class because my hair will frizz on the walk from the valet to the front door.
A clear sign from the universe that I should order Thai food and remain under a weighted blanket until spring.

prompt: What is the primary function of a mirror during a workout?

options.label: To critically evaluate my form while subtly checking if my pump makes me look intimidating.
To take a post-class selfie with a subtle filter that highlights my clavicle and my expensive water bottle.
Mirrors are an abomination. I don't want to look at myself, and I certainly don't want anyone else looking at me.
To practice my 'I'm thinking deeply about my life choices' face while trying to catch my breath after climbing one flight of stairs.

prompt: An enthusiastic instructor yells, 'Come on, push through the pain, you're doing this for YOU!' directly into a headset microphone. How do you respond?

options.label: I find their forced optimism deeply patronizing and would pay good money to never hear their voice again.
I am fueled entirely by their validation. If they don't look at me and say 'Good job, Sarah!' my workout is a failure.
I can't hear them over the sound of my own internal grunting and the noise-canceling headphones blaring deathcore.
I close the laptop screen or lie down on the floor, pretending I have suddenly developed a medical emergency.

prompt: Someone has been sitting on the only bench press rack for 15 minutes, doing exactly one set and scrolling through Instagram for the remaining 14. Your strategy:

options.label: Stand exactly three feet away from them, glaring with arms crossed, establishing raw territorial dominance.
This is why I bought my own equipment. Society is fundamentally broken.
Kindly ask the front desk coordinator to intervene because confronting strangers ruins the studio's collective aura.
Use their bad etiquette as a valid excuse to abandon my workout entirely and head straight to the snack bar.

prompt: When choosing what to wear for physical exertion, your primary criteria is:

options.label: Does it match the current color palette of my Instagram feed, and did it cost more than a week of groceries?
An oversized, frayed t-shirt from a 2012 high school charity drive and whatever sweatpants don't have structural holes.
Brand new, high-end athletic wear that still has the tags on it because I'm manifesting a lifestyle I haven't started yet.
Whatever is cleanest on top of the laundry basket. Nobody is seeing me anyway except my cat.

prompt: The workout is over. What are you putting into your body to recover?

options.label: A lukewarm shaker bottle containing chalky whey protein powder mixed with tap water. Performance over pleasure.
A $16 artisanal cold-pressed beverage featuring adaptogenic mushrooms, blue spirulina, and oat milk.
Whatever leftovers are closest to expiration in my fridge, consumed standing up over the sink.
A massive serving of complex carbohydrates and refined sugars because 'I earned it' by thinking about walking on a treadmill.

prompt: The workout facility requires finding parking in a crowded downtown area during rush hour. Your psychological state is:

options.label: Unbothered. The road rage simply adds to my pre-workout adrenaline rush.
This is a structural failure of modern urban planning. I will remain in my home where parking is guaranteed.
I pay for the premium tier membership that includes complimentary valet parking, so it's a non-issue.
I'm turning the car around immediately. Finding a spot is too stressful, and stress kills more people than lack of cardio.

prompt: How do you feel about making eye contact with another human being while making an unattractive face during physical exertion?

options.label: Let them look. If they can't handle me at my absolute ugliest lifting heavy weights, they don't deserve me at my baseline.
I would rather swallow a live wasp than have another person see my face while I struggle.
The lighting is dimmed to a subtle neon glow precisely so no one can see individual suffering. It's a shared aesthetic vibe.
I don't make unattractive faces because I never exert myself to the point of discomfort.

prompt: Where does your motivation to exercise primarily originate?

options.label: A deep, dark, unresolved psychological need to prove my high school gym teacher wrong.
The fear of wasting the ridiculous amount of money I spent on a boutique subscription package this month.
A structured, unemotional habit built into my daily routine like brushing my teeth. Motivation is for amateurs.
It doesn't. It exists briefly for about 10 minutes after watching an inspiring documentary, then completely vanishes.

prompt: What is the soundtrack to your physical suffering?

options.label: Curated, high-tempo electronic dance music blasted through a million-dollar club-grade sound system.
Aggressive, unhinged heavy metal or gangster rap that makes me want to fight a wall.
True crime podcasts where people get brutally murdered in the woods. It keeps me focused.
The sweet sound of a television comedy playing in the background while I check my phone every 45 seconds.

prompt: You finish using a piece of equipment and notice a slight sheen of your own sweat on it. What is your process?

options.label: Thoroughly sanitize it with the provided disinfectant spray, ensuring the next person doesn't contract my DNA.
I don't wipe down anything because it's my house, my sweat, and my kingdom of dirt.
The dedicated studio staff or the next person will handle it; my premium fee includes maintenance.
I walk away slowly, hoping no one saw me, and never return to that specific side of the room.

prompt: It is January 2nd. The fitness space is absolutely packed with eager new faces. Your internal monologue says:

options.label: Look at these fragile tourists. They’ll all quit by February 15th anyway. Stay out of my squat rack.
Thank God I don't have to deal with this seasonal migration pattern in my spare bedroom.
Oh wow, everyone looks so cute in their new matching sets! The energy in this community is so vibrant right now!
I am one of these eager new faces, and I am already deeply regretting every financial and physical choice that led me here.

prompt: You encounter a complex fitness machine with multiple pulleys, levers, and an instruction diagram that looks like a NASA blueprint. You:

options.label: Figure it out by trial and error, loading up the weight stack until it makes a satisfying clanking noise.
Avoid it completely. If it can't be done with a heavy iron ball attached to a handle, it's unnecessary commercial fluff.
Wait for an instructor to precisely calibrate the settings for me so I don't compromise my alignment or break a nail.
Stare at it blankly for 30 seconds, sit on it backwards by accident, get embarrassed, and go look at the water fountain.

prompt: To cancel your membership, you must send a notarized letter via carrier pigeon to an office in North Dakota. Your response:

options.label: I will never cancel. This place is my temple, and my membership card will be buried with me.
I don't sign contracts with corporate overlords. I pay cash for my iron or use gravity for free.
I just use a boutique class pack that handles everything seamlessly through an app with zero friction.
I will simply accept the recurring charge for the next five years because confronting administrative friction is terrifying.

prompt: Your smartwatch dies 2 minutes into your workout. What happens next?

options.label: The workout is officially void. The calories didn't count, the heart rate wasn't logged, and my day is ruined.
Who cares? The heavy things are still heavy regardless of what a piece of wrist plastic thinks.
Perfect. Now I can sit down and stop moving entirely since there's no digital record to hold me accountable.
I keep moving according to my internal training log. I don't need a cloud server to validate my suffering.

prompt: What does your ideal exercise environment smell like?

options.label: A delicate blend of premium lavender essential oils, expensive cleaning chemicals, and high-end air purifiers.
Industrial rubber flooring, stale chalk, dried sweat, and a subtle undertone of raw, unadulterated effort.
Dust motes, laundry detergent from the adjacent washing machine, and complete social isolation.
Scented candles, freshly baked goods from the kitchen, and absolutely zero physical exertion.

prompt: Everyone in the room is performing a complex explosive movement that looks highly dangerous for your lower back. You:

options.label: Do it anyway because the peer pressure in this room is intoxicating and I want the instructor to like me.
Modify it immediately to a basic heavy compound lift. I don't do circus tricks for clout.
I am not in the room. I am alone in my house doing basic movements where no one can judge my safety metrics.
Slowly lay down on a mat, hold my hamstring, and make a pained face so people think I’m an injured athlete instead of lazy.

prompt: It's Saturday at 8:00 AM. Your alarm goes off. What is your reality?

options.label: I'm already at the studio door, waiting for the doors to open so I can secure my favorite Reformer machine.
Crawling out of bed to lift heavy iron before the commercial gym gets overrun by teenagers filming their biceps.
Drinking coffee in my underwear while staring at the pull-up bar I installed in the doorway, mentally preparing to use it.
Smashing the snooze button with the force of a thousand suns and waking up at noon with a headache.

prompt: The lighting in your ideal workout facility should be described as:

options.label: Harsh, unyielding fluorescent bulbs that show every single flaw, vein, and drop of sweat with clinical accuracy.
A dark, nightclub-adjacent aesthetic with neon accents that make everyone look 40% more attractive than they actually are.
The natural, low-wattage lighting of my own home where I can control the blinds and hide from the public.
Completely off. The only light should be coming from a television screen while I lie horizontal.

prompt: How much financial skin do you need in the game to actually take your health seriously?

options.label: $10 a month. If it costs any more, it's a scam. The iron doesn't care about luxury branding.
At least $300 a month. If the price tag doesn't cause me slight financial panic, I simply will not show up.
Zero. I bought my weights once, and I will use them until the day I die without paying rent to a facility.
It doesn't matter how much I spend; I will consistently pay the premium and still find a reason to stay home.

prompt: When you see an acquaintance at the fitness venue, your immediate internal reaction is:

options.label: Put on my oversized headphones, pull down my hat, and pretend to be illiterate and blind.
Wave enthusiastically, plan to get a post-workout brunch together, and spend 10 minutes discussing our weekend plans.
Give a brief, silent head nod of mutual athletic respect, then immediately return to my training set.
Hide behind a pillar, wait for them to leave, and text them later saying 'So bummed I missed you, I worked out so hard!'

prompt: You wake up the morning after a workout and your thighs feel like they are made of shattered glass. You think:

options.label: This is a magnificent sensation. The biological machinery is rebuilding. Time to train upper body.
I need to book a professional sports massage and buy an expensive topical recovery cream immediately.
Hobble over to my foam roller in the living room, curse quietly to myself, and stick to the routine.
I have clearly done permanent structural damage to my anatomy. I am retired from physical activity indefinitely.

prompt: What is your preferred method of elevated cardiovascular activity?

options.label: Staring at a blank wall on a commercial treadmill for 45 minutes while pondering the futility of human existence.
Sprint intervals down my own street or high-intensity jumping jacks in my kitchen where nobody can watch me bounce.
An immersive, rhythm-based choreography experience where the lights flash and I forget I'm actually doing cardio.
Walking slowly to the kitchen to see if the contents of the refrigerator have magically changed since I checked 10 minutes ago.

prompt: Your phone's home screen has a dedicated folder filled with 7 different fitness tracking apps. How often do you use them?

options.label: Every single day to log my exact sets, reps, and micro-progression metrics.
Only when I need to book a highly competitive reservation slot for a premium studio class.
They exist purely as a digital monument to my good intentions. I open them once every quarter to feel guilty.
I don't use apps. I write my numbers in a battered, sweat-stained paper notebook like a real lifter.

prompt: Choose your primary weapon for maintaining optimal cellular hydration:

options.label: A massive, unbranded 1-gallon plastic jug filled with tap water that looks like it belongs on a construction site.
A highly trendy, insulated tumbler in a limited-edition pastel colorway that matches my outfit.
A standard reusable bottle covered in stickers from independent outdoor brands, filled with filtered home water.
An open can of diet soda or a sugary iced coffee that defeats the entire biological purpose of hydration.

prompt: What is the perfect amount of time to allocate to a single exercise session?

options.label: Exactly 45 minutes of hyper-efficient, high-octane group movement followed by a quick exit.
A brutal 90-minute marathon of lifting, resting, staring blankly, and lifting again.
A swift, convenient 25-minute circuit done during a lunch break before my coworkers realize I'm gone.
About 12 minutes of light stretching followed by 50 minutes of lying on the mat scrolling through real estate apps.

prompt: Your psychological preparation routine before physical exertion consists of:

options.label: Consuming enough highly caffeinated pre-workout powder to make my skin itch and my heart beat like a techno song.
Drinking a double espresso in my own kitchen while quietly staring at the wall in absolute silence.
Applying a spritz of expensive body mist and selecting a premium hair tie to ensure complete aesthetic synergy.
Spending 45 minutes looking for my running shoes, giving up, and deciding that today is a rest day.

prompt: You walk into a new fitness facility for the first time. Your dominant emotion is:

options.label: Excitement to survey the equipment landscape and evaluate the heavy-duty rack options.
Deep admiration for the interior design, the complimentary beauty products, and the retail selection in the lobby.
Intense social dread. I don't belong here, everyone is watching me, and I want to escape back to my cave.
Pure resentment that I was dragged here by a friend when I could be at home consuming media.

prompt: You experience a minor tweak in your left wrist. How does this impact your long-term fitness strategy?

options.label: Modify my programming to focus entirely on lower body exercises. The grind doesn't stop for a weak joint.
Freeze my boutique membership immediately and notify my instructor that I will be absent for rehabilitation.
Accept my fate as a housebound creature, focusing on core exercises that require zero upper body engagement.
This is a medical catastrophe. I must remain completely immobile on the sofa for the next six months to ensure full recovery.

prompt: Your workout partner texts you at 5:30 AM saying, 'Hey, sorry, can't make it today.' Your immediate action is to:

options.label: Go anyway. My dedication was never contingent on their fragile work ethic.
Thank the fitness deities for this absolute gift, roll back over, and sleep for another two hours.
Pivot to a solitary home session where I don't need human validation to execute my programming.
Feel intense panic because I don't know what to do without a group environment guiding my every move.

prompt: If you had to choose a professional to direct your physical movement, you would choose:

options.label: A former drill sergeant who communicates entirely in guttural shouts and demands absolute physical submission.
A charismatic fitness influencer who tells me I'm a radiant warrior while adjusting my pelvis posture.
No one. I am my own master, guided entirely by YouTube instructional videos played at 1.5x speed.
A passive-aggressive therapist who tells me it's okay to give up and accept my physical mediocrity.

prompt: Your opinion on the presence of complimentary dry shampoo, luxury body lotion, and premium hair straighteners is:

options.label: An absolute operational requirement. Without them, the facility is essentially a medieval dungeon.
A complete waste of capital. Spend that money on better barbells and thicker rubber matting.
Irrelevant because I use my own bathroom facilities immediately after completing my session.
I enjoy looking at them while avoiding the actual exercise portion of the facility visit.

prompt: Two individuals are aggressively high-fiving and grunting after a heavy lift. Your psychological assessment is:

options.label: Beautiful. Pure communal camaraderie. I want to join their pack and share their chalk.
An exhausting display of toxic performance. This is why I refuse to leave my house.
Deeply uncivilized. They would never survive the polite, synchronized etiquette of a premium Pilates studio.
A fascinating anthropological display that I will judge harshly while eating a pastry in my car.

prompt: You are staying at a hotel for a three-day vacation. The hotel fitness center consists of two rusty dumbbells and a broken bicycle. You:

options.label: Find a local hardcore powerlifting gym within a 20-mile radius and pay the exorbitant day-rate fee.
Perform a high-intensity bodyweight routine on the hotel carpet, ignoring the questionable stains beneath my face.
Locate the nearest luxury studio branch to maintain my class streak and secure an aesthetic vacation selfie.
Celebrate this structural limitation as a legitimate excuse to consume alcohol at the hotel bar starting at 11:00 AM.

prompt: When is it appropriate to document your physical state via a photographic digital asset?

options.label: Only when the lighting hits my musculature at a precise 45-degree angle, demonstrating functional hypertrophy.
Before, during, and after every single class, complete with geo-tags and a mention of the premium studio brand.
Never. The camera is an instrument of vanity and I am a ghost operating in silence.
Only when I want to post a throw-back picture from three years ago to pretend I still possess physical vitality.

prompt: The workout is complete. It is time for the cool-down and stretching protocol. Your implementation looks like:

options.label: Walking directly to the exit. I don't have time to touch my toes; I have macro-nutrients to consume.
An intensive, 15-minute meditative yoga flow in my living room with the lights dimmed and incense burning.
Enjoying a complimentary chilled eucalyptus towel placed over my face while lying in a state of luxury bliss.
Sitting on the floor, stretching my lower back for 20 seconds, and immediately scrolling TikTok for the next half hour.

prompt: You inherit $2,000 that must be spent exclusively on your physical well-being. You allocate it to:

options.label: A commercial-grade squat rack and a premium barbell that will outlive my grandchildren.
A multi-year membership at a hardcore warehouse gym that smells like iron and competitive drive.
A premium package of 50 VIP passes to an exclusive boutique studio with private locker privileges.
An incredibly comfortable ergonomic lounge chair designed to optimize my passive relaxation intervals.

prompt: You arrive at your exercise destination and realize you forgot your headphones. Your psychological resolution is:

options.label: Suffer through the awful corporate pop music playing over the facility speakers; the weights are still heavy.
Turn around and go home. Exercising without my precise internal audio narrative is a structural impossibility.
Irrelevant. The studio instructor curates a custom live DJ set for every session, so I don't bring headphones anyway.
Go home and spend the rest of the day carefully curating a new playlist that I will never actually exercise to.

prompt: What is the primary aesthetic or physical goal you are trying to achieve through this suffering?

options.label: To look like an intimidating brick house capable of moving a broken vehicle out of an intersection.
To possess a lean, functional body that can survive a sudden zombie apocalypse without leaving my property.
To look exceptionally sleek in high-end leisure apparel while walking through a premium grocery store.
To accept my soft, pillowy form as a silent protest against the aggressive demands of the fitness industry.

prompt: An acquaintance posts a photo of their shredded abdominal muscles on vacation. Your immediate internal reaction is to:

options.label: Mute their account immediately because their aggressive success highlights my complete lack of discipline.
Analyze their vascularity to determine if their training protocol involves more compound lifting than mine.
Leave a supportive comment asking what premium studio method they're currently utilizing to achieve those results.
Ignore it entirely while closing the app to order another plate of fried carbohydrates.

prompt: You realize you haven't done laundry and your only available fitness clothes are slightly damp and smell faintly of yesterday's effort. You:

options.label: Put them on anyway. The bacteria builds character and the gym environment is already filthy.
Cancel my scheduled studio class because showing up smelling less than pristine is a social felony.
Work out naked or in my undergarments in the privacy of my own bedroom where laundry metrics don't apply.
Use this laundry crisis as an unassailable moral justification to abandon all physical movement for the week.

prompt: You are 4 minutes into a steady-state cardio session and you feel like your brain is slowly leaking out of your ears from boredom. You:

options.label: Embrace the void. Suffering is the point. Focus entirely on the rhythmic thumping of my feet.
Step off the machine, log out of the interface, and accept that I am simply not a cardio person.
Switch to an interactive digital landscape app that pretends I'm running through the Swiss Alps away from a bear.
I don't experience this because the instructor keeps changing the intervals every 30 seconds while shouting inspirational quotes.

prompt: A personal trainer with a clipboard and an aggressive smile approaches you to offer a 'complimentary metabolic assessment.' Your defensive mechanism is:

options.label: Look them dead in the eye and state my exact current deadlift metrics until they realize they have nothing to teach me.
Lie and tell them I am moving to another continent tomorrow to avoid any further sales conversations.
Politely inform them that I already have an elite private coach who manages my wellness matrix.
This encounter is exactly why I pay to stay behind a deadbolted door in my own residential property.

prompt: When you buy new fitness equipment or memberships, what are you actually purchasing?

options.label: Access to heavy-duty machinery capable of facilitating maximal mechanical tension.
The privilege of being left completely alone to suffer in an environment designed for efficiency.
A temporary hit of dopamine that allows me to pretend I'm an active person without doing any actual work.
An entry ticket into an exclusive social community of high-status, attractive individuals.

prompt: You decide to 'clean up your diet.' Your approach can be best described as:

options.label: Buying $200 worth of organic green juices and artisanal salads that will rot in the crisper drawer while I eat pizza.
Calculating my exact macronutrient targets down to the single gram and meal prepping unseasoned chicken breasts.
Subscribing to an expensive premium meal-delivery service that arrives in a insulated tote bag with ice packs.
Eliminating a single random food item (like seed oils) while completely ignoring my massive caloric surplus.

prompt: Be brutally honest with yourself: If nobody knew you worked out, and it didn't change your physical appearance, would you still do it?

options.label: Yes. The physical challenge and the routine keep me sane. The iron is my meditation.
Absolutely not. If I can't look good in clothing or post about it, I am staying on the couch forever.
Yes, because I genuinely enjoy the process of optimizing my biological machinery in absolute privacy.
I don't even do it now when people DO know and it DOES change appearances, so the question is entirely academic.

prompt: It is Tuesday at 6:45 PM. It is dark, slightly drizzling, and you just finished a brutal work meeting. The gym is a 12-minute drive away. What are you doing?

options.label: Driving there. The iron calls to me.
Sitting in my car in the driveway for 25 minutes scrolling TikTok, eventually eating cereal.
I'd only go if it's a candlelit room where someone yells at me to cycle to a remix.
Going straight into the garage in my pajamas to swing a kettlebell so I don't have to perceive society.

prompt: Be honest: what does your gym membership history actually look like?

options.label: I've been going consistently for years. It's basically my third home.
I signed up in January, went twice, and I'm still paying for it in July.
I canceled my membership and bought dumbbells instead. Best decision of my life.
I only do class packs at the studio with the good playlist and the eucalyptus towels.

prompt: How do you feel about taking a gym selfie mid-workout?

options.label: Sweaty, red-faced, mid-rep, no shame. That's the whole point.
I only take one if the lighting, outfit, and smoothie are all coordinated.
Absolutely not. No one is allowed to witness me exist while exercising.
I take the selfie before I even work out, then never actually work out.

prompt: You feel a tiny twinge in your knee. What happens next?

options.label: I keep lifting. Pain is just weakness leaving the body, probably.
This is now my official reason for not exercising for the next 6 months.
I switch to a gentle at-home mobility routine and actually listen to my body.
I book a 'recovery' Pilates class because it sounds soft and expensive.

prompt: How much are you realistically willing to spend per month on 'getting fit'?

options.label: $25-40. Just give me a squat rack and leave me alone.
$0 after the initial equipment purchase. I refuse to pay monthly for anything.
$150-300. I need the ambiance, the instructor, and the branded water bottle.
Whatever amount guarantees I feel guilty enough to maybe go once.

prompt: It's 5:30 PM, peak hour, every squat rack is taken, and someone's doing curls in it. Your reaction?

options.label: I wait, I superset, I adapt. That's just gym life.
This is exactly why I built a home gym. No waiting, no strangers, no curls in the rack.
I'd rather pay for a class with assigned spots so this never happens to me.
I take this as a sign from the universe to go home and skip it.

prompt: What's actually playing while you 'work out'?

options.label: Aggressive rap or metal, loud enough to justify my grunting.
A curated remix an instructor picked, synced perfectly to the choreography.
A true crime podcast, alone, in silence otherwise.
Whatever's playing on the TV I'm watching instead of working out.

prompt: How do you feel about communal locker rooms and showers?

options.label: Fine. It's a means to an end, I'm there to lift, not socialize.
Absolutely revolting. I will shower in my own home like a civilized person.
Only acceptable if it has nice tile, good products, and no fluorescent lighting.
Irrelevant, since I've never actually made it past the parking lot.

prompt: What actually gets you to push through the last rep?

options.label: My own internal rage and a heavy bar loaded with plates.
A perky instructor screaming 'you got this queen' over a bass drop.
Absolute silence and the knowledge that no one is watching me struggle.
Nothing, because I stopped at rep one and started a snack instead.

prompt: What's your go-to post-workout refuel?

options.label: A shaker bottle of plain whey protein, chugged in the parking lot.
A $14 smoothie with adaptogens, collagen, and a name like 'Glow Getter'.
Water from my own fridge, in peace, while nobody watches me chug it.
A celebratory donut because I 'earned it' by thinking about exercising.

prompt: What does your current home 'gym' situation look like?

options.label: A full rack, barbell, plates, and a pull-up bar. It's basically a real gym.
One yoga mat still in its original packaging, under the couch.
Nothing, because I need someone to hand me equipment and validation simultaneously.
Nothing at home, I need a heavy rack and other people around to feel like I'm really lifting.

prompt: What does your 'rest day' actually look like?

options.label: Active recovery, foam rolling, maybe a light walk. I still respect the process.
A gentle stretch class with soft music and someone telling me I'm doing great.
Every day is rest day. That's not a joke, that's my lifestyle.
Lying on my garage floor after a workout, alone, refusing to move or speak to anyone.

prompt: How do you feel about fitness trackers and workout apps?

options.label: I track sets, reps, and weight religiously in a spreadsheet like a scientist.
I have five fitness apps installed and zero workouts logged in any of them.
I only care about the app if it lets me book a cute class and pick my bike.
I use a private app at home so nobody can see my numbers or my form.

prompt: It's freezing and raining sideways outside. Does your workout plan change?

options.label: Nope, the gym has a roof and heating, I'll be fine.
Not at all, my garage doesn't care about weather and neither do I.
This is the perfect excuse to skip everything and stay under a blanket.
I'll still go if the studio has heated floors and hands me a towel at the door.

prompt: Do you need a workout buddy to actually show up?

options.label: No, I lift alone. Other people just slow down my supersets.
I need a whole class of strangers cheering so I feel like I'm at a party.
I need a buddy so badly that when they cancel, I cancel too, forever.
Absolutely not, I actively avoid workouts that require me to talk to anyone.

prompt: What are you wearing to 'work out' today?

options.label: Old gray shorts, a stained tank top, and lifting shoes that have seen things.
A perfectly color-coordinated set I bought specifically for this one class.
Whatever pajamas I already have on. Nobody's grading my outfit in my garage.
Cute workout clothes I bought to feel motivated, that I will now wear to get coffee instead.

prompt: How do you feel about deadlifts specifically?

options.label: It's the greatest lift ever created and I will die on this hill.
I do them alone with a barbell in my garage, no chalk board audience needed.
Too scary, too heavy, too loud. I'd rather do a barre pulse to a pop song.
I've seen a video of one once and that felt like enough exposure.

prompt: How much does fitness influencer content actually change your behavior?

options.label: Not really, I have my program and I'm sticking to it regardless of trends.
I buy the exact leggings and book the exact class they're always in.
I watch, I save the video, I never do the workout, and I feel accomplished anyway.
I copy their home workouts exactly, alone, with zero desire to be seen doing them.

prompt: How often do you cancel workout plans at the last minute?

options.label: Rarely. If it's on my calendar, I'm going, full stop.
Constantly, and I have an increasingly elaborate excuse ready every time.
Only if there's no cute outfit or good class available that day.
I can't cancel because it's just me, a kettlebell, and my own conscience.

prompt: What's your ideal workout ambiance?

options.label: Chalk dust in the air, weights slamming, someone grunting three racks over.
Dim lighting, candles, a diffuser, and a playlist that sounds like a spa.
Total silence except my own breathing and the hum of my garage fridge.
The ambiance of my living room, TV on, snacks within arm's reach.

prompt: How do you actually track whether you're improving?

options.label: I track weight on the bar every single session. If it's not going up, I'm mad.
I track how the class made me feel and whether the instructor remembered my name.
I track my own reps and times privately, no need for anyone else to know.
I track how many days it's been since I last thought about working out.

prompt: How do you feel about running?

options.label: On a gym treadmill, in the cardio section, next to everyone else's treadmill.
Only in a dark, sweaty spin class with lasers and a DJ.
Alone on a trail or in my basement, absolutely nobody around to see my form.
I've considered it, in theory, as a concept, once, briefly.

prompt: How do you feel about signing a 12-month gym contract?

options.label: Sign me up, I know I'll actually use it every week.
I'd rather buy equipment once and never deal with a contract again.
I only want drop-in class packs, no commitment, just vibes.
I'll sign it, feel amazing for one day, and never think about it again.

prompt: What's your actual pre-workout routine?

options.label: A scoop of pre-workout, headphones in, don't talk to me.
Picking the perfect outfit and checking the class roster to see who's teaching.
Locking the garage door so no one can witness my questionable form.
Scrolling my phone until the 'ideal window' to work out has definitely passed.

prompt: How do you feel about asking a stranger to spot you on a heavy lift?

options.label: No problem, gym etiquette exists for a reason, we help each other out.
Never, I'd rather fail alone in my garage than ask a stranger for help.
I'd rather have an instructor gently adjust my form during a class.
Irrelevant, since heavy lifting isn't really part of my current lifestyle.

prompt: After a long work day, how much social interaction can you handle during a workout?

options.label: A moderate amount, gym small talk is fine, I just need my headphones as an escape hatch.
Zero. I need total isolation or I will not move a single muscle.
I actually crave it, I want a whole room of people high-fiving me.
None, because social interaction is exactly why I'm on the couch instead.

prompt: Think back to your first real workout experience. What happened?

options.label: I got hooked on lifting immediately and never really left the gym since.
I loved a group class so much I signed up for a package on the spot.
I hated everyone watching me and swore to only ever exercise alone from then on.
I got so sore I decided this whole 'fitness' thing wasn't for me.

prompt: What's your real, honest fitness goal right now?

options.label: Add more weight to the bar. Get stronger. Simple as that.
Feel calm, private, and in control without anyone else around.
Feel like I'm part of a cute community with good energy and better lighting.
Feel like I'm 'about to start' something, indefinitely, without starting anything.

prompt: You open a fitness booking app. What happens next?

options.label: I book the class with the cutest branding and the best smoothie bar.
I don't need an app, I know my program and my gym's hours by heart.
I close it and scroll something else instead for the next two hours.
I never open apps like this, I have my own routine at home, no booking required.

prompt: A friend invites you to a 6 AM boot camp class this weekend. Your response?

options.label: Sure, as long as it's real weights and not just jumping around to pop music.
Absolutely, I'll bring my cutest set and tell everyone about it after.
Hard pass, I don't do group activities before 9 AM or ever.
'Maybe!' I say, already knowing I will sleep through my alarm on purpose.

prompt: If you actually did the math, how much is each of your workouts costing you?

options.label: Very little, since I go 4-5 times a week and use every dollar of my membership.
Zero, since I already own everything and paid once, years ago.
Around $35 a class, but the smoothie and the vibe make it worth it.
I genuinely don't want to know how much per visit I'm paying for zero visits.

prompt: What's actually on your TV right now while you 'plan' to work out?

options.label: Nothing, I'm at the gym, not sitting on a couch near a TV.
A workout video I follow along to, alone, in my living room.
A cooking show, while I eat something that is definitely not a smoothie.
An influencer's vlog of their studio workout that I will not personally replicate.

prompt: How do you feel about the specific smell of a commercial gym: rubber mats, chalk, and industrial cleaner?

options.label: It smells like productivity. I love it, honestly.
Disgusting. My garage smells like nothing, which is exactly how I like it.
I'd only tolerate it if it were replaced with eucalyptus and essential oils.
No idea, I've never actually been inside long enough to smell anything.

prompt: Does seeing someone lift more than you at the gym bother you?

options.label: It fuels me. I'm adding weight to the bar next set, guaranteed.
I only compete with the leaderboard on my own equipment, alone, at home.
I only care if I got more 'high fives' from the instructor than the person next to me.
I wouldn't know, I've never been in a room where lifting was happening.

prompt: How does your meal prep situation match your workout intensity?

options.label: Chicken, rice, broccoli, in bulk, every single week, like clockwork.
I make my own smoothies and snacks at home, no need to buy them anywhere.
I buy the $16 acai bowl instead of making anything myself, obviously.
My meal prep is ordering delivery and calling it 'self-care'.

prompt: What time do you actually, realistically, work out?

options.label: 5:30 AM, before the gym gets crowded, every single day.
Whenever, since my garage is open 24/7 and no one's judging my schedule.
Only for the specific class time slot I already booked and paid for.
'Tomorrow.' The time is always tomorrow.

prompt: Someone offers to film your set to check your form. How do you feel?

options.label: Great, send it, I want the feedback so I can lift heavier and safer.
No thank you, my form is a private matter between me and my garage walls.
Only if it's for a cute Instagram Reel with good lighting and a caption.
There's nothing to film, since there's no actual movement happening.

prompt: You're on vacation for a week. What happens to your workout routine?

options.label: I find the closest hotel gym and squeeze in a session anyway.
I do bodyweight circuits in my hotel room, alone, curtains closed.
I book a fancy studio class local to wherever I'm traveling, obviously.
What routine? I've been 'on vacation' from working out for a while now.

prompt: What does your supplement shelf actually look like?

options.label: Whey, creatine, a shaker bottle with mystery residue. Functional and unglamorous.
Collagen powder, adaptogenic mushrooms, and something with rose quartz infused water.
A basic protein tub I bought online, used at home, no fanfare required.
An unopened multivitamin bottle from three years ago that I take as proof I 'tried'.

prompt: How do you feel about a group fitness class in general?

options.label: Only if it's strength-focused and no one's asking me to 'find my inner light'.
Love it. I need the choreography, the community, and the playlist to feel alive.
No thanks, group anything sounds like my personal nightmare.
I'd love it in theory, but I've never actually attended one.

prompt: How long is your ideal workout session?

options.label: 60-90 minutes. I need warm-up sets, working sets, and a cooldown.
45 minutes, exactly the length of the class I already paid for.
20-30 minutes, efficient, private, and over before anyone notices I did anything.
Zero minutes, but I will think intensely about working out for a full hour.

prompt: You had an awful day. What actually happens to your workout plan?

options.label: I go anyway. Lifting heavy things is how I process my emotions.
I want a soft, guided class where someone tells me it's okay to feel this way.
I retreat to my own space and move quietly, alone, no witnesses needed.
I use it as the perfect justification to eat my feelings on the couch instead.

prompt: What's the current status of your gym bag?

options.label: Packed, by the door, ready to go, lifting belt included.
I don't need one, my equipment lives permanently in my garage.
A cute tote with a grip sock, towel, and my class schedule inside.
Somewhere in my closet, unopened, tags still on from a purchase two years ago.

prompt: Given a choice, heavy weights and low reps, or light weights and lots of reps to music?

options.label: Heavy, low reps, obviously. Go big or don't bother showing up.
Light weights, high reps, to a great beat, with an instructor counting for me.
Whatever I already own at home, alone, at my own pace.
Neither, since 'weights' aren't really part of my daily vocabulary.

prompt: Do you 'check in' on social media when you work out?

options.label: No, I'm too busy actually lifting to post about lifting.
Absolutely, tagged location, sweaty selfie, smoothie in hand, the whole aesthetic.
Never, nobody needs to know what happens in my garage.
I post an inspirational quote about fitness instead of actually doing any fitness.

prompt: What does your warm-up actually look like?

options.label: Light cardio, mobility drills, then a couple warm-up sets before the real work begins.
A guided warm-up flow, led by an instructor, set to gentle music.
A few stretches on my own living room floor, then straight into it.
Stretching my arm to reach the remote counts, right?

prompt: The day after a hard workout, how sore are you and how do you handle it?

options.label: Extremely sore, and I wear it like a badge of honor at the office.
Sore in a cute, humble-braggy way I mention in the class group chat.
Sore, but I deal with it quietly and alone with a foam roller.
Not sore at all, since soreness requires actually doing the workout first.

prompt: Realistically, where do you see your fitness routine one year from now?

options.label: Same gym, more weight on the bar, same grind, just stronger.
A more elaborate home setup, still solo, still zero interest in gym crowds.
A loyal member of my favorite studio, on a first-name basis with the instructors.
Exactly where I am now, with a slightly more expensive unused membership.

prompt: Last question. Deep breath. What's the real, unfiltered truth about your relationship with exercise?

options.label: I genuinely love the grind, the iron, and the routine. It's non-negotiable for me.
I only move my body when it feels aesthetic, social, and expensive enough to matter.
I'll do the work, but only if absolutely no one else is around to witness it.
I like the idea of fitness a lot more than I like actually doing it.

prompt: Select the option that matches your current relationship with your gym card:

options.label: It is currently acting as a structural shim to level out my kitchen table.
I keep it in my wallet to trick myself into thinking I have a social life.
The gym staff recently threw me a surprise party because they forgot what I looked like.
I go regularly, mostly to sit in the massage chairs and use their high-speed Wi-Fi.
